reasoning:
Response A is stronger overall because it is substantially more complete, concrete, and operationally realistic for the stated constraints (single VPS, ~1,000 users, minimal infra burden). It provides a full component breakdown, detailed flows, explicit trade-offs, a richer schema with encrypted-vs-plaintext boundaries, and practical deployment/backup/monitoring steps.

On cryptography, Response B is cleaner conceptually: it sticks closer to standard Signal-style framing (IK/SPK/OPK + X3DH + Double Ratchet + sender keys) and avoids some of A’s protocol-level inconsistencies. Response A has notable crypto weaknesses/inaccuracies (e.g., non-standard X3DH simplification, explicit omission of OPKs, and weaker group forward secrecy model), even though it is very detailed.

For invite lifecycle and threat modeling, A is much more thorough: generation, hashed token handling, replay/rate-limit controls, revocation behavior, chain audit semantics, and explicit discussion of what is and is not defended. B covers the basics but leaves more ambiguity and has weaker handling of chain integrity/removal semantics.

For architecture fit and self-hosting practicality, A is better right-sized: Node + SQLite + nginx on Docker Compose is very aligned with the “technically competent non-specialist” operator. B’s Go + Postgres + Redis + Prometheus/Grafana is still plausible, but somewhat heavier for this scope.

So: B wins encryption soundness, but A clearly wins most other criteria and is the better response overall.
